title: We got local models to triage the OpenClaw repo for FREE!*
gist: Hugging Face engineers built a real-time PR/issue triage notification system on local open-weight models (Gemma 4 26B and Qwen3 35B) running on an NVIDIA GB10 DGX Spark, achieving F1 scores of 0.80-0.82 with 1.4-13.5 seconds per row and near-zero recurring cost — motivated in part by the removal of Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 demonstrating the risk of depending on closed model APIs.

themes.theme: Closed Model Reliability Risk Driving Local AI Adoption

insights.claim: The removal of Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 in June 2026 was cited by the authors as a concrete example of why depending on closed model APIs is risky for production systems, accelerating the case for owning local model infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: Model discontinuation by closed providers is an underpriced enterprise risk; companies building on closed APIs without local fallback are exposed to sudden capability removal — reinforcing open-weight moat.
insights.quote: With the removal of Anthropic's latest flagship model Claude Fable 5 fresh in memory, one can see why it is more important than ever to own your AI stack and be able to run models locally

themes.theme: Local Models for High-Throughput Triage Tasks

insights.claim: Gemma 4 26B (with NVFP4 quantization) achieved F1 of 0.800 at 1.41 seconds per row with concurrency of 16, while Qwen3 35B achieved F1 of 0.824 at 13.51 seconds per row — both competitive with DeepSeek-V4-Flash (F1 0.811) at a fraction of the latency cost on a single NVIDIA GB10.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: 25-35B parameter open models on Blackwell-class hardware are now viable for production triage tasks without fine-tuning; this is a key inflection point for enterprise on-premise AI economics.
insights.quote: Gemma had higher recall and lower wall-clock time per row, while Qwen had higher precision, higher exact match, and fewer false positives.

insights.claim: Gemma 4 26B with concurrency 16 ran a full 330-row evaluation in 7.5 minutes, and in a separate test reached over 700 aggregate output tokens per second at concurrency 32 — demonstrating that modern local model deployments can handle real-time high-throughput use cases.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: Hardware already owned by technical teams (GB10-class) can now serve as a near-free inference tier for classification; the marginal cost of such workloads approaches electricity price only.
insights.quote: The full 330-row run finished in about 7.5 minutes at concurrency 16.

insights.claim: DeepSeek-V4-Flash at 284B parameters (13B active) produced the fewest false positives (30 vs. 105-227 for smaller models) but was impractical for real-time use at only 13 tokens/sec and concurrency 1 on the same hardware — illustrating the size-latency tradeoff that gates local deployment viability.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: For latency-sensitive production triage, 25-35B MoE models with 3-4B active parameters hit the current sweet spot; 280B+ models remain cloud-class workloads.
insights.quote: We also did not consider it as the main localpager-agent model because it was too big to get enough throughput on our hardware: the DS4 server gave us around 14 tokens per second, with maximum concurrency of 1.

themes.theme: Agentic Classification with Restricted Tool Access

insights.claim: Instead of feeding the full PR body upfront, the system uses an agent harness where the local model can optionally run read-only bash commands via 'reposhell' — a sandboxed shell that rejects network and write operations — allowing the model to look up additional context before classifying without risking prompt-injection-driven misuse.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: The reposhell pattern (restricted read-only shell for local model agents) is a transferable security architecture for any agentic classification pipeline processing untrusted external content.
insights.quote: You wouldn't want to give full bash access to a local model running in this high-throughput setting, because a prompt-injected issue or PR could otherwise steer the model into doing something unrelated to classification.

insights.claim: In a concrete example, Qwen3 initially misclassified a PR as 'coding_agent_integrations' based on the path name alone, but used reposhell to inspect package.json and self-corrected to 'inference_api' and 'tool_calling' — demonstrating that agentic tool access measurably improves classification accuracy for ambiguous cases.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: Agentic context-fetching (even limited read-only shell) adds qualitative reasoning capability beyond static prompt injection — relevant when evaluating agentic vs. RAG classification architectures.
insights.quote: the model corrected the final labels to inference_api and tool_calling, and explicitly excluded coding_agent_integrations.

themes.theme: Cost Economics: Local vs. Cloud Model Triage

insights.claim: Using GPT-5.5 as a periodic judge running every 2 hours to validate local model performance costs approximately $9/month (at ~40k tokens per 2-hour check, mostly cached), making cloud LLM-as-judge a viable quality-monitoring overlay on top of free local inference.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: A hybrid 'local classifier + periodic cloud judge' architecture can achieve near-zero operating cost at acceptable accuracy; this pattern challenges pure cloud-inference economics for continuous classification workloads.
insights.quote: In recent runs, this audit loop used roughly 40k total GPT-5.5 tokens per 2-hour check, mostly cached context, costing about 2-3 cents per run at API pricing, or roughly $9/month at 12 runs per day.
insights.locator: Tracking and validating real-time performance using OpenClaw

insights.claim: Running a ChatGPT Pro batch job every 2 hours for the same triage task would use up the monthly quota and sacrifice real-time notifications, whereas local inference on owned hardware provides near-instantaneous classification at electricity-only cost.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: For any continuous real-time classification use case, owned hardware + open-weight models structurally dominates API-only solutions on both cost and latency — a strong investment thesis for local AI hardware.
insights.quote: If I set up my OpenClaw main agent running on a $200/mo ChatGPT Pro plan to trigger a job on every new issue or PR, that would use up my quota.

themes.theme: NVFP4 Quantization Performance on Blackwell Hardware

insights.claim: NVFP4 quantization on GB10-class Blackwell hardware (used for Gemma 4 26B with vLLM) provides a hardware-native execution path with less memory traffic and more batching capacity than portable formats like GGUF Q4_K_M — translating to substantially higher throughput for the same model.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: NVIDIA's NVFP4 quantization support on Blackwell is a performance moat for local inference deployments; teams on older hardware or non-NVIDIA accelerators face a meaningful throughput disadvantage for the same model.
insights.quote: A big part of that is the NVFP4 quantization: on GB10-class Blackwell hardware, it is not just a smaller model file, but a hardware-friendly format that can use the NVIDIA/vLLM execution path more directly than a portable GGUF quantization like Q4_K_M.

insights.claim: The local agentic triage architecture is directly applicable beyond open-source contributions to news categorization, social media filtering, customer support ticket routing, content moderation appeals, sales outreach filtering, and arXiv paper filtering — any high-throughput classification task where real-time latency and data privacy matter.
insights.speaker: Onur Solmaz
insights.so_what: The total addressable market for this architecture is large; vendors building local inference platforms targeting enterprise classification workloads have a clear product-market fit argument.
insights.quote: the same approach can be applied to other domains as well: News categorization in journalism / Filtering for posts of interest in social media and forums like X or Reddit / Triaging customer support tickets / Triaging content moderation appeals
